title: Trump says 'good' talks ongoing with Iran but offers few details
summary: If talks did take place, it is not clear how serious any US-Iranian negotiations have been, whether the US and Israel will continue other airstrikes and whether Iran is still restricting access to the Strait of Hormuz - the reason for Trump's Monday bombing deadline in the first place.

title: China pledges more balanced trade and further opening of the economy after record surplus
summary:
China will import more high-quality foreign goods and work with all ‌parties to promote optimised and balanced trade development and expand the global trade pie, Li told the China Development Forum in Beijing, according to state media.
It comes after the world's second-biggest economy reported a record $1.2 trillion trade surplus for 2025.
In a separate speech at the forum, China's central bank governor Pan Gongsheng also sought to alleviate concerns surrounding the trade surplus.
China has no need and no intention to gain trade competitive advantage through currency depreciation, Pan said.
FOREIGN INVESTMENT PUSHChina is working to reverse a decline in foreign direct investment, which fell 5.7% year-on-year to just over 92 billion yuan ($13.36 billion) in January, following a 9.5% drop over the course of 2025.

title: Oil falls and shares rebound after Trump says talks have been held to end war
summary: "No negotiations have been held with the US, and fakenews is used to manipulate the financial and oil markets and escape the quagmire in which the US and Israel are trapped," Mohammad-Bagher Ghalibaf, speaker of Iran's parliament wrote on X.

title: Iran war enters its fourth week with no clear end in sight
summary:
Iran war enters its fourth week with no clear end in sighttoggle caption Amir Levy/Getty Images EuropeAs the war in the Middle East enters its fourth week, President Trump said on Friday the administration is considering "winding down" military efforts in the region.
The Pentagon also says it is targeting the small, fast boats that Iran uses in the Persian Gulf.
Trump administration to lift sanctions on Iranian oil stranded at seaThe U.S. is temporarily lifting sanctions on some oil from Iran.
This new lifting of sanctions applies to Iranian oil and oil products that are currently loaded on ships.
Turkey said the attacks this week on Syria, its neighbor, were illegal.

title: Iran threatens to 'completely' close Strait of Hormuz and hit power plants after Trump ultimatum
summary:
Iran said the Strait of Hormuz, crucial to oil and other exports, would be "completely closed” immediately if the U.S. follows up on President Donald Trump's threat to attack its power plants.
The aims have ranged from weakening Iran's nuclear program, missile program and support for armed proxies to enabling the Iranian people to overthrow the theocracy.
Under international law, power plants that benefit civilians can be targeted only if the military advantage outweighs the suffering it causes them, legal scholars say.
Advertisement AdvertisementTehran praised its attack as a show of strength, even as Israel's military asserts that Iranian missile launches have decreased since the war began.
Advertisement AdvertisementLebanese authorities say Israel's strikes have killed more than 1,000 people and displaced more than 1 million.

title: BBC in Cuba as island plunged into darkness
summary:
More than 10 million people in Cuba have had power cut to their homes and businesses after the country's national electrical grid collapsed.
The Caribbean island has been hit with several blackouts this month, as a result of a US fuel blockade that cuts off the foreign oil imports needed to keep power stations running.
The Communist-run country has an ageing electricity infrastructure and chronic fuel shortages.
The BBC's Will Grant reports from the capital Havana.

title: Will the Houthis join Iran in war against Israel and the US?
summary:
The Yemeni armed group says all options are on the table.
As the US-Israeli war against Iran drags on, Yemen’s Ansar Allah, or the Houthis, have stayed out of the conflict.
They have said they consider themselves directly concerned and could take a position alongside Iran.
The armed group has attacked Israel and shipping in the Red Sea in recent years.
So, will the Houthis join the war?

title: Iran war: What’s happening on day 22 of US-Israel attacks?
summary:
In IranCasualties: The war has killed 1,444 people in Iran, including at least 204 children.
The war has killed 1,444 people in Iran, including at least 204 children.
Bahrain reports that it has destroyed a total of 143 missiles and 242 drones since Iranian attacks began on February 28.
Saudi forces said they shot down at least 47 drones, including a concentrated barrage of 38 drones within just three hours.
Saudi forces said they shot down at least 47 drones, including a concentrated barrage of 38 drones within just three hours.

title: ‘They want to colonise us’: Brazil’s Lula warns of foreign interference
summary:
Brazilian President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva has criticised what he called the return of a colonial approach towards developing nations during a summit in Colombia.
“It’s not possible for someone to think that they own other countries,” Lula said, in an apparent reference to US policy.
“They want to colonise us again.”The left-wing Brazilian president also criticised the ongoing war launched by the US and Israel against Iran.
Advertisement“Where are Saddam Hussein’s chemical weapons?” Lula asked.
While large-scale, overt US involvement in the region mostly petered out after the Cold War, Trump has rekindled the legacy.

title: Iran says will hit region’s energy sites if US, Israel target power plants
summary:
Iran’s parliament speaker warns country could ‘irreversibly destroy’ vital infrastructure across the region after Trump threatens to attack power plants if Strait of Hormuz is not opened.
Iran has threatened to hit energy sites in the Middle East after US President Donald Trump threatened to attack the country’s power plants if Tehran does not open the strategic Strait of Hormuz.
Critical ⁠infrastructure ⁠and energy facilities in the region could ⁠be “irreversibly destroyed” should Iranian power plants be ⁠targeted, Iran’s Parliament Speaker Mohammad Bagher Ghalibaf posted on ‌X on Sunday.
His comments came after Trump on Saturday said the US would “obliterate” Iran’s power plants if it did not open the Strait of Hormuz within 48 hours.
In a statement, the IRGC said that ⁠companies with ⁠US shares will be “completely destroyed” if Washington targets Iranian energy facilities, and that energy facilities in countries that host US ‌bases ‌will be “lawful” targets for Iran.

title: Drone strike near Iraqi intelligence headquarters in Baghdad kills officer
summary:
One police officer killed in strike by ‘outlaw groups’ on headquarters of Iraqi National Intelligence Service.
One police officer has been killed in a drone strike by “outlaw groups” on the headquarters of the Iraqi National Intelligence Service in the heart of capital Baghdad.
“A drone targeted the headquarters of the Iraqi National Intelligence Service in the Mansour district” at about 10am local time (07:00 GMT), General Saad Maan, head of the Iraqi government’s security media unit, said in a brief statement on Saturday.
Another drone, filming the operation, crashed into a private members ‘ sports club popular with the Iraqi elite and foreign diplomats, according to the same source.
The drone attack on the headquarters of the National Intelligence Service came hours after another attack on the US military complex.

title: Saudi Arabia expels Iran military attache, four embassy staff
summary:
Saudi Arabia has given Iran’s military attache and embassy staff 24 hours to leave the kingdom due to “repeated Iranian attacks” on its territory.
Among the attacks, energy facilities in the east of Saudi Arabia have been repeatedly targeted, as well as the capital, Riyadh, where the US embassy was hit by two drones earlier this month.
The port is the only export outlet for Saudi Arabia after Iran effectively blocked tanker traffic leaving the Gulf via the Strait of Hormuz.
The foreign minister said that Saudi Arabia and other Gulf states had “very significant capacities and capabilities that they could bring to bear should they choose to do so”.
On Saturday, the Foreign Ministry said that continued Iranian attacks would lead to further escalation and have “significant consequences” for current and future relations.

title: Power without exit – America’s strategic trap in the Strait of Hormuz
summary:
Even limited disruption can trigger global economic tremors, placing pressure not only on the United States but on its allies and adversaries alike.
The United States, once the architect of multilateral action, now finds itself acting alone, its calls for support met with hesitation or silence.
As James M. Dorsey astutely observes, “it takes three to tango.” The United States is not the sole protagonist.
Caught between these two actors, the United States finds itself in a reactive posture.
For the United States, anything short of clear dominance risks being perceived as defeat.

title: Pakistan T20 cricket league to be held in empty stadiums amid oil crisis
summary:
Pakistan Super League will be held on schedule from March 26, but without the crowds and only at two venues.
Pakistan’s premier domestic T20 league will take place in empty stadiums due to the recent spike in oil prices, says a top official of the Pakistan Cricket Board (PCB).
Pakistan has faced soaring oil prices prompted by the United States-Israel war on Iran and the subsequent spread of the conflict across the region.
“We can’t ask people to restrict their movements and then have 30,000 people in stadiums every day.
Naqvi apologised to the four cities – Rawalpindi, Faisalabad, Multan and Peshawar – that will no longer host PSL games this season.

title: Israel Launches Fresh Wave of Attacks on Iran as Rift With Trump Grows
summary:

By 7:10 AM ET, the S&P 500 surged +240 points adding +$2 TRILLION in market cap,” the analysis noted.

the S&P 500 had fallen -120 points erasing -$1 trillion in market cap.

title: The First Casualty of Trump’s War in Iran Was the Truth
summary:
Now, as war has engulfed both the region and the global economy, Trump and his sycophantic advisers have taken to improvising on the fly, floating conflicting justifications for war and predictions about its duration.
When confronted with these contradictions and falsehoods, all the President’s men followed his lead: they blamed the media.
With increasing frequency, Trump berates reporters (particularly female reporters).
The owner of the Washington Post, the newspaper of Watergate, has done irreparable violence to his property merely to stay in Trump’s good graces.
In Washington these days, there is hardly a reporter who does not have the President’s cellphone number.

title: From the 1956 Suez Crisis to the Hormuz Crisis. Repeating History from Hungary to Taiwan? - Global Research
summary:
The Suez Crisis began on July 19, 1956, when the United States withdrew financing for the Aswan Dam in Egypt.
Israel was the trigger of the 1956 Suez intervention.
The foundations of this capability were laid in the late 1950s, directly following the 1956 Suez Crisis.
In this sense, the Suez Crisis was not just a regional conflict; it was the decisive turning point that enabled Israel’s emergence as a nuclear power.
Western Pacific and Taiwan Crisis PotentialThe Hormuz crisis hits the Indo-Pacific region the most today.

title: What Would an Abundance Foreign Policy Look Like?
summary:
As would-be policymakers peer ahead and imagine a world beyond Donald Trump, an abundance foreign policy could help sharpen their vision for the future.
An abundance foreign policy must therefore emphasize strategic and largely nonideological geoeconomic statecraft in seeking to further U.S. growth.
An abundance foreign policy would ask of every major international affairs decision: Does it expand the U.S. capacity to build without sacrificing core security and values?
The first priority of an abundance foreign policy should be to expand and diversify supply chains.
An abundance foreign policy points to a wider set of intended beneficiaries—not only workers in exposed industries but anyone squeezed by high prices and scarcity.
